Assumption:<[silence:100]> "Amputees ‘imagine’ pain where limbs used to be."
Reality:<[silence:100]> The brain doesn’t hallucinate—it rewires, hijacking sensory cortex real estate (Ramachandran, 1998).<[silence:400]> When nerves sever, they scream in silent Morse code:<[silence:100]> 70% of phantom pain sufferers report "cramping" in fingers they no longer have (Journal of Pain, 2015).<[silence:400]> Your body isn’t lying—it’s archiving the wound.<[silence:400]>

You’ve spent years adapting—but what if your brain’s ghost isn’t a glitch, but a ledger of everything you refused to mourn? Which loss are you still paying for in flesh that isn’t there?
clean_story:
Phantom Pain Isn’t a Ghost—It’s a Map of What You Lost.

Assumption: "Amputees ‘imagine’ pain where limbs used to be."
Reality: The brain doesn’t hallucinate—it rewires, hijacking sensory cortex real estate (Ramachandran, 1998). When nerves sever, they scream in silent Morse code: 70% of phantom pain sufferers report "cramping" in fingers they no longer have (Journal of Pain, 2015). Your body isn’t lying—it’s archiving the wound.

Assumption: "Time heals phantom pain."
Reality: Mirror therapy (tricking the brain with reflections) works in 60% of cases—but only if you confront the void daily (Lancet, 2007). Avoidance? That’s how pain becomes permanent, a neural scar tissue. The limb is gone; the memory of control isn’t.

Unlearning Challenge:
You’ve spent years adapting—but what if your brain’s ghost isn’t a glitch, but a ledger of everything you refused to mourn? Which loss are you still paying for in flesh that isn’t there?
